title: Listera ovata
intro: Listera ovata, also known as twayblade or yellow adder's mouth, is a terrestrial orchid species native to Europe, North Africa, and temperate Asia. It has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including inflammation and respiratory problems, due to its potential to reduce oxidative stress and improve immune function. The bioactive constituents of L. ovata include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, L. ovata is often prepared as a decoction or tincture, with the most common preparations including teas and infusions to treat coughs and colds.
taxonomy: Listera ovata is classified as a species of orchid, specifically within the subfamily Orchidoideae. It belongs to the family Orchidaceae. This orchid is further classified as a member of the genus Listera, which includes several other species. Listera ovata is commonly known as the twayblade and is native to Northern Europe, Western Asia, and North America. Within the genus Listera, L. ovata is often grouped together with other species, such as L. cordata and L. australis.
uses: Listera ovata, also known as the twayblade orchid, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant's roots are said to have anti-inflammatory properties, which may help alleviate symptoms of arthritis and other inflammatory conditions. Additionally, the roots are believed to have antiseptic and antibacterial properties, making them useful for wound care and treating infections. Some studies suggest that Listera ovata may also have antioxidant and anti-cancer properties, although further research is needed to confirm these findings. However, it is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using the plant for medicinal purposes, as its effectiveness and safety have not been thoroughly scientifically proven.

side_effects: Listera ovata, also known as the twayblade, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. However, there is limited research on the side effects of consuming Listera ovata. Some reports suggest that it may cause gastrointestinal issues, such as nausea and vomiting, in high doses.
